posts.title: Write a Cover Letter That Gets Read
posts.caption:
Your cover letter has about 8 seconds.

Three paragraphs. One hook that's specific to this company. One paragraph of matched evidence. One confident close.

Most letters get cut because they could have been sent to any company. The ones that get read couldn't.

Swipe for the full framework - structure, opening line formula, tailoring method, and before/after examples.

Find roles worth applying for at internwise.co.uk
captionThread: Your cover letter has 8 seconds. Most recruiters don't finish them. Here's exactly what makes the ones that get read different. A thread.
Three paragraphs. That's it. Opening hook specific to this company. One paragraph matching your evidence to the JD. A confident close. Nothing else needs to be there.

The opening line is the only one that matters. Don't start with 'I am writing to apply.' Start with the one thing that connects you to this specific role - a project, a result, a reason that couldn't apply to any other company.
Para 2 is your evidence paragraph. Pick 3 skills from the job description. Match each one to something real you've done. No soft claims. No 'I am passionate about.' Just evidence.
Para 3 closes the loop. Restate your interest in one sentence. Say you'd welcome the chance to discuss. Sign off. Under 350 words total - one full page maximum.

The before/after is stark. Generic: 'I have always been passionate about finance.' Tailored: 'Your graduate scheme's rotation structure is exactly the kind of breadth I've been building toward - here's why.' One gets read. One doesn't.

Five checks before you hit send: opening line is specific to this company - para 2 matches 3 skills from the JD - under 350 words - company name and role spelled correctly - read it aloud. If it sounds stiff, rewrite it. Find roles worth applying for at internwise.co.uk

posts.title: 5 Interview Questions Every Recruiter Asks
posts.caption:
Everyone gets nervous before an interview. But the 5 questions every recruiter asks? You can prepare for those.

Past - Present - Future. Research - Align - Aspire. Real - Addressed - Growing. STAR. Company - Growth - Honest.

The frameworks that make the difference between stumbling and standing out.

Swipe to nail every one.

Find roles worth preparing for at internwise.co.uk
captionThread: 5 questions every recruiter asks in every interview. Here are the exact frameworks to answer each one.
Q1: "Tell me about yourself." Use Past - Present - Future. Start where you've been. Land where you're going. Keep it under 90 seconds. Practice it out loud until it flows.
Q2: "Why do you want to work here?" Research - Align - Aspire. If your answer works for any company, you haven't done enough research. Name something specific about this one.
Q3: "What's your biggest weakness?" Real - Addressed - Growing. Name an actual weakness. Tell them what you're actively doing about it. Never say 'I'm a perfectionist.'
Q4: "Tell me about a time you..." STAR method. Situation - Task - Action - Result. Use 'I', not 'we'. Quantify the outcome if you can. Prepare 5 of these before any interview.
Q5: "Where do you see yourself in 5 years?" Company - Growth - Honest. Show you want to develop here. Name specific skills. You don't need a 5-year plan. Show ambition and curiosity.
What every interviewer is actually scoring: Clarity (can you think under pressure?), Self-awareness (do you know yourself?), Fit (does your story match what they need right now?).
The best answers are prepared, not improvised. Find roles worth preparing for at internwise.co.uk

posts.title: Why 80% of CVs Never Reach a Human (ATS Guide)
posts.caption:
Most CVs never reach a human.

Not because you're underqualified. Because a piece of software filtered you out first.

Tables, columns, graphics, non-standard headings, missing keywords. Each one is a reason to be rejected before anyone reads your name.

The fix takes less than an hour. Swipe.

Find roles at internwise.co.uk
captionThread: Most CVs are rejected before a human reads them. Not because of experience. Because of formatting. Here's what's getting you filtered out.
What is ATS? Applicant Tracking Systems scan every CV submitted online. They score you on keywords, structure, and format. Below the threshold, your CV is never seen.
ATS killer #1: Wrong format. Tables and columns scramble the output. Headers and footers are ignored. Graphics disappear. Use a single-column, plain text layout. Every time.
ATS killer #2: Missing keywords. If the job description says 'Data Analyst', don't write 'Data Specialist'. Copy the exact phrases from the job description - that's what ATS searches for.
ATS killer #3: Non-standard headings. ATS looks for 'Work Experience', 'Education', 'Skills'. Not 'My Journey' or 'Things I've Built'. Be completely literal with section titles.
ATS killer #4: Fancy formatting. Progress bars, logos, coloured boxes, skill chips. All of them confuse the parser. Plain text. Bold for headings. Bullet points only. Nothing else.
The ATS-proof checklist: .docx format / single column / standard headings / keywords from the JD / no graphics / no text boxes. Six rules. Every application, every time.
A clean CV gets read by a human. That's the only goal. Find roles worth applying for at internwise.co.uk

posts.title: Graduate Schemes vs Direct Entry
posts.caption:
1-3% acceptance rate. Applications open September to January only. 9-12 months before you start.

That's a graduate scheme.

Direct entry at an SME: higher acceptance rate, year-round applications, 4-8 weeks to start.

Neither path is wrong. The question is which one fits where you are right now.

Most graduates only look at one. Most opportunities are on the other.

Find both at internwise.co.uk

Sources: ISE Graduate Recruitment Survey 2025/26, High Fliers Graduate Market Report 2026

posts.title: How to Build Experience When You Have None
posts.caption:
'No relevant experience' is the most common reason graduates don't apply.

It's also mostly wrong.

You can build real, CV-worthy experience right now. Without connections. Without a placement already lined up.

Build something. Volunteer for responsibility. Do a micro-internship. Take one freelance job. Build your presence online.

5 methods. Any of them will move your application from 'no relevant experience' to 'here's what I've done'.

Swipe.

Find entry-level roles at internwise.co.uk
captionThread: Everyone says you need experience. Here's how to get it when you have none. 5 methods that actually work.

Method 1: Build something. A project beats a blank CV every time. Aspiring marketer? Write a newsletter. Developer? Build an app on GitHub. Analyst? Clean a public dataset and write it up. One finished project beats 10 courses.

Method 2: Volunteer for responsibility. Not just to attend - to run something. Treasurer, events lead, sports captain. Budget management, coordination, team leadership. It's real experience. Name it properly on your CV.
Method 3: Micro-internships. Short, project-based, paid roles - usually 1-4 weeks. Low barrier. Real company names on your CV. Do 2-3 back to back and your experience section looks very different.

Method 4: Freelance, even once. Find one small business that needs a task done. Social content, data work, writing, or design. Write it as 'Freelance [role] - self-employed'. It is legitimate work experience.

Method 5: Build your online presence. A complete, keyword-rich profile, a GitHub with clean projects, a personal site showing your work. You don't need thousands of followers. You need to be findable.
You don't need experience to start. You need to start to get experience. Find your first role at internwise.co.uk

posts.title: Graduate Salaries by Sector 2026
posts.caption:
What UK employers actually pay you in 2026.

We broke down graduate starting salaries across every major sector - from £22K in hospitality to £65K+ in finance and law.

The wild card? A supermarket grad scheme that beats most finance roles on base salary. Not what you expected.

Swipe to see where your sector lands.

Find roles matched to your salary at internwise.co.uk
captionThread: What UK employers actually pay graduates in 2026. Starting salaries broken down across every major sector. The range is wider than most people realise. Here's the full picture.
Hospitality and retail: £22K-£28K. The lowest-paid sector for graduates. High street roles often start near minimum wage. But top retail grad schemes can reach £40K+.
Marketing, HR and comms: £25K-£29K. Below the graduate average to start. Digital marketing roles pay better (£27K-£49K). Pick the right company and progression is fast.
Tech, engineering and consulting: £30K-£55K. The widest range of any sector. Startups start around £28K. Large tech firms go to £55K base. AI Engineer is the fastest-growing grad title in 2026.
Finance, law and accounting: £35K-£65K+. The highest ceiling. Top City law firm trainees start at £56K. Large accounting firms start £28K-£33K but the long-term ceiling is genuinely high.
The wild cards: £40K-£52K+. Not finance. Not law. A leading supermarket grad scheme pays £52,020 + a company car. Intelligence services: £40K-£44K. Not what most people expect.
The number to know: £30,500. That's the broader graduate market average. Top 100 employer median is £35K. Never accept below the market average without a clear reason.
Now you know the numbers. Find roles matched to your target salary at internwise.co.uk

posts.title: UK Grad Job Market 2026
posts.caption:
The UK grad job market in 2026 is brutal. But most people are fighting the wrong battle.

140 applications per vacancy. 7% fewer jobs. Sounds bad.

But entry-level only dropped 6% while big schemes fell 10%. SMEs hire year-round. AI and tech are growing fastest.

The gaps exist. You just have to know where to look. Swipe.

internwise.co.uk
captionThread: The UK grad job market in 2026 is brutal. But here's exactly where the gaps are. A thread on what the data actually says.
140 applications per vacancy. 7% fewer grad vacancies. Sounds terrible. But these numbers hide the real picture.
Entry-level roles fell just 6%. Big corporate schemes fell 10%. The competition is concentrated at the big names. Entry-level is where your opportunity is.
Three sectors are actively hiring grads right now: AI and technology (fastest growing), health and life sciences, infrastructure and engineering.
60% of grad applications go to London. But London holds less than half the vacancies. Same roles. Half the competition. Applying only to London makes it harder than it needs to be.
SMEs hire year-round. No September deadline. No 6-stage process. Most grads ignore them completely. That's your edge.
44% of Gen Z say not having the right network is their biggest barrier to getting hired. You can build one. It just has to be intentional.
Stop fighting for the same 100 roles as everyone else. The gaps exist. You just have to know where to look. internwise.co.uk

posts.title: Turn Your Internship Into a Job Offer
posts.caption:
Most interns finish and leave. The ones who got return offers did 3 things differently.

It's not about being the smartest in the room. Not about staying late every night.

It's about treating the internship like a 10-week interview - and playing it accordingly.

Swipe for the full playbook.

Find internships worth converting - internwise.co.uk
captionThread: Most interns finish and leave. Here's what the ones who got return offers did differently. A playbook in 3 steps.
Thing 1: treat it like a 10-week interview. Not a CV builder. Not work experience. An audition. Every interaction counts.
Thing 2: own one project. Deliver it visibly. Don't wait to be assigned credit. Make sure the right people see the outcome.
Thing 3: build relationships up and across. Not just your manager. The people two levels up. The people in other teams. Conversations, not just connections.
Most interns ask the wrong question. Not 'Can I have a job?' but 'What would I need to demonstrate for a return offer?' Then go and demonstrate it.
What most interns do: turn up, do the work, say goodbye. And wonder why the offer didn't come.
A return offer isn't luck. It's a playbook. Find internships worth converting. internwise.co.uk
